wheelDepth.s4: Prioritize, policy-design, and formulate defensive/adaptive action responses.

bcDepth.title: Robinson's 4-Step Backcasting Framework

citations.portalBody:
Eight teaching modules in this portal:
1. FITness — René Rohrbeck (Corporate Foresight, 2010)
2. CLA — Sohail Inayatullah (Metafuture)
3. Futures Wheel — Jerome C. Glenn (Millennium Project)
4. Backcasting — John B. Robinson (Energy Policy)
5. Popper Diamond — Rafael Popper (EU FORLEARN)
6. Six Pillars — Andy Hines & Peter Bishop
7. Scenario Planning — Shell / Peter Schwartz
8. Futurpreneurship — foresight × venture
citations.fitnessBody: This assessment module is based on documented research by Professor René Rohrbeck at EDHEC Business School. Benchmark studies and findings on the 33% profitability advantage of foresight-driven organizations are available at rene-rohrbeck.com and his indexed publications on ResearchGate.
citations.claTitle: Academic Sources for Causal Layered Analysis:
citations.claBody: Causal Layered Analysis (CLA) was developed by distinguished futurist Sohail Inayatullah. His theoretical and workshop case studies—including the reference book The Causal Layered Analysis Reader and articles on CLA 2.0 and 3.0—are indexed on metafuture.org and the USC Research Bank.
citations.wheelBody: The Futures Wheel methodology was first introduced by Jerome C. Glenn in 1971. Comprehensive guides on probability calculations and quantitative consequence mapping appear in Futures Research Methodology 3.0 by The Millennium Project, with the full chapter also available on ResearchGate.
citations.backcastBody: Normative backcasting was first formalized by John B. Robinson in 1982 for energy policy planning. Key works include Energy Backcasting: A Proposed Method of Policy Analysis in the journal Energy Policy, along with evolutionary participatory backcasting in sustainability literature. Urban resilience principles based on backcasting are also cited in foresight studies at the University of Copenhagen.

aboutPage.title: About me
aboutPage.mission:
I hold a PhD in Futures Studies from the University of Tehran, with a focus on strategic foresight, emerging technologies, and uncertainty-driven decision systems.

My work is centered on understanding technological change before it becomes obvious — through weak signals, scenario planning, and system-level thinking. My deeper specialty is Type-2 fuzzy logic: modeling cases where uncertainty about uncertainty matters.

Professionally, I also work in cloud and infrastructure engineering. I build and operate systems using tools like Docker, Kubernetes, and modern DevOps practices.

I do not see these as separate domains. Foresight and infrastructure are both about systems — just at different time scales.
aboutPage.closingNote: I am not trying to predict the future. I am trying to understand how it becomes inevitable.

articleRohrbeck.title: Why Rohrbeck FITness Still Matters
articleRohrbeck.subtitle: Future Readiness is not enough; organizations must perceive, prospect, and probe.
articleRohrbeck.body:
In management meetings we often hear “we have a foresight unit” or “our Future Readiness score is high.” Then a crisis hits and the same monthly reports land on the table. The brutal question follows: why does your foresight fail at the moment of decision?

Many organizations discover this is not a lack of reports. Reports exist, workshops happened, slides were shown. The problem is that foresight has been documentation, not live capability. It resembles shallow country comparisons: they look scientific until a crisis exposes the missing context.

René Rohrbeck’s Future FITness model draws the line. Readiness means “we are prepared.” FITness means “we are actively adjusting to the future.” The gap sounds subtle; in practice it is enormous. One produces documents; the other tests new paths.

when foresight is only performance

In many firms, foresight becomes an annual event: a trend report, a few AI slides, applause, and a return to the same operating loop. Perceiving, Prospecting, and Probing are missing. There is “seeing” without exploring or probing.

Cases at Siemens and Deutsche Telekom show foresight lives when tied to technology radar, venturing, and investment decisions. When it only decorates board meetings, it becomes beautiful, convincing, and useless.

three processes, five dimensions

Perceiving means catching weak signals and structural shifts. Prospecting means exploring possible paths, not one linear forecast. Probing means pilots, ventures, and small experiments that test the future instead of only talking about it.

Five maturity dimensions (culture, structure, process, resources, output) separate reporting theater from real capability. Without foresight culture, the best report goes to the shelf. Without venturing structure, probing cannot happen. One weak dimension collapses the whole system into performance.

alive or documented?

FITness still matters because it answers the opening question. If your foresight only works in calm times and goes silent in crisis, you have Readiness, not FITness. If the team reports but nothing reaches the market as a probe, your foresight is documented, not alive.

Ask yourself: when did foresight last change a real decision? If the answer is hard to find, the problem is not tools; it is architecture. FITness is a navigation map, not a decorative label.

articleCla.title: What CLA Sees Before Policy
articleCla.subtitle: Before policy comes discourse and myth; CLA makes them visible.
articleCla.body:
A new policy is announced. A task force forms, data is collected, a document is drafted. Yet some options never reach the table. Even with “scientific” analysis, the debate returns to the same menu. Why?

That question is the heart of CLA. Sohail Inayatullah’s Causal Layered Analysis shows that policy takes shape in deeper layers before any clause is written. If you only see the surface, it is like reading a scoreboard and thinking you know the whole game.

the Litany trap

Policymakers and media usually work at the Litany layer: numbers, crises, headlines, KPIs. That layer matters, but if we stop there, deeper causes and hidden worldviews stay invisible. Litany tells you what happened, not why only certain options feel thinkable.

Think of claims that “sport must stay separate from politics” while the framing, naming, and staging of an event is itself precise social engineering. The surface looks neutral; deeper layers already defined the game.

four layers, one picture

Systemic opens structures: law, markets, infrastructure, incentives. Worldview opens assumptions: “growth is always good,” “the state must control,” “technology fixes itself.” Myth/Metaphor opens the stories that limit debate: hero, victim, golden return, absolute binaries.

CLA says that until metaphor shifts, the policy menu does not shift. You can tweak numbers, but if the myth remains “all or nothing,” middle paths never become serious.

In Singapore and ASEAN, CLA reshapes conversation before the policy paper. When stakeholders move from Litany to Worldview and Myth, options appear that once seemed impossible. Here foresight and policy truly connect.

a pre-policy tool

CLA is a pre-policy tool. It explains why some options never reach the table, not which option is “correct.” If you want genuinely new policy, first see which discourse and myth already constrain it.

Ask: when did a policy meeting last discuss the Myth layer? If you always stay in Litany and Systemic, do not expect breakthrough. CLA is a flashlight for the layers that define the game before the law is written.

articleUncertainty.title: Navigating Uncertainty
articleUncertainty.subtitle: When we are unsure how sure we are, we need different tools.
articleUncertainty.body:
Two experts treat a weak signal seriously; two others say it is too early. Management wants one number, one decision, one direction. What do you do? Vote and move on?

This happens in foresight more often than we admit. Scenarios and critical uncertainties are powerful, but they hide an assumption: boundaries can be fixed. When judgments themselves are fuzzy, forcing a single probability is a strategic error.

manufacturing false certainty

In foresight sessions, a number often lands on the table: “0.7 probability,” “high confidence trend,” “medium-priority weak signal.” It looks scientific. Behind it, real expert disagreement is hidden. The person who said 0.7 might accept 0.4 to 0.8; the dissenter might dispute the definition of “emerging.”

That disagreement is part of reality, not method failure. In deep uncertainty, the boundary between “important” and “noise,” “likely” and “unlikely,” is itself fuzzy. Ignore that and your foresight becomes shallow simulation: a model that misrepresents the world.

Type-2 fuzzy logic

Lotfi Zadeh introduced Type-1 fuzzy logic for degrees of membership: a trend can be “somewhat” emerging. Type-2 goes further: it models uncertainty about the fuzzy boundaries themselves. Instead of one crisp membership function, you get a Footprint of Uncertainty (FOU); the model admits “we do not know exactly where the boundary is.”

In foresight this means saying “this trend is likely emerging” with a band of doubt, not a single 0.7 everyone must accept. For weak signals, expert disagreement, and decisions under ambiguity, that is often more honest than naive probability.

Example: a new AI technology. Team A sees a strong signal; Team B sees noise. Type-2 lets you say membership is high, but uncertainty about membership is also high. Management understands it should probe, not commit all-in.

do not hide ambiguity

Navigating uncertainty means choosing tools that do not fake certainty. The opening question remains: when even “how sure are we?” is disputed, the answer is the right tool.

If your foresight always delivers one clean number, it probably killed ambiguity instead of resolving it. In a world where weak signals matter, honesty about uncertainty beats fake precision. Type-2 fuzzy logic is technical language for a honesty that management meetings often lose.

articleAiTime.title: Beyond the Biological Clock
articleAiTime.subtitle: Time in the body, the organization, and the machine; three rhythms, not one.
articleAiTime.body:
A manager wants a large language model live next month. Engineering says weeks. Legal says years. HR says the workforce is not ready. Who is right?

If you accept only one timeline, someone must be “wrong.” But everyone may be right; they are talking about different clocks. That is where technology foresight often fails: forcing one biological clock on every team.

one calendar for humans and machines

Human circadian rhythm is slow and cyclical: focus, fatigue, weekly cadence. AI systems and cloud infrastructure count milliseconds. Organizations sit between: committees, approvals, budget cycles. Treat the three as one and you build lying roadmaps.

Many tech analyses compute “time to market” from technical latency alone. Social adoption, regulatory change, and organizational learning run on separate clocks. It is like saying that because the steam engine existed, whole societies should industrialize within a year.

three kinds of time in one analysis

In technology foresight, “time” is not just a timeline. Decision tempo (how fast you can commit), infrastructure latency (how fast you deploy), and social adoption rhythm (how long people and institutions accept change) are separate layers.

LLM deployment is a clear example: technically possible in weeks, pilot in months, compliance and trust in years. Fuzzy logic can model “slow,” “gradual,” and “sudden” without hard cutoffs; not a crisp 18 months, but a band from 6 to 36 with different confidence per layer.

If your foresight only sees the manager’s clock, engineering always looks slow and legal always looks heavy. Separate the three clocks and you see where the bottleneck is and where to place the probe.

hold several rhythms at once

Beyond the biological clock means seeing technical, organizational, and social time separately, then combining them. The opening question is answered: both sides can be right; they speak of different clocks.

For every emerging technology project, ask three questions: how long technically? how long for the organization to accept? how long for society and regulation? Answer only one and your foresight is incomplete. The future of technology is multi-temporal; foresight must be too.

articleHinesBishop.title: From Toolbox to Process
articleHinesBishop.subtitle: Hines and Bishop’s Six Pillars answer “what do I do now?”
articleHinesBishop.body:
FRM 3.0 offers 39 methods. A manager looks at the list and asks: where do I start? Run everything at once and foresight becomes a heavy, exhausting project; run nothing and it stays talk. That paradox is real for many organizations.

It resembles copying another country’s development model without asking which stage you are in and where the bottleneck sits. Plenty of tools, little architecture; the result is exhaustion or theater.

the toolbox trap

FRM 3.0 is a treasure chest: Delphi, scenarios, futures wheel, CLA, backcasting, and dozens more. A chest becomes a problem when you do not know which key opens which lock. Many organizations start with one big workshop, produce 200 pages, and never reach Acting.

Great Scanning without Visioning yields shelf reports. Strong Visioning without Acting yields management wallpaper. Forecasting without Framing answers a question nobody asked. Every FRM method has value, but without architecture it is random.

six pillars, one path

Andy Hines and Peter Bishop turned foresight into a process: Framing → Scanning → Forecasting → Visioning → Planning → Acting. Each pillar has a failure mode and a blunt question.

Framing: “Are we asking the right question?” Scanning: “Do we see the horizon or only read feeds?” Forecasting: “Do we have plausible futures or fantasy?” Visioning: “Do we share an image?” Planning: “Do we have a pathway?” Acting: “Has a probe reached the market?”

The Cone of Plausibility bridges Forecasting and Visioning; not every fantasy, only plausible futures. In Houston’s program and APF courses, once an organization knows which pillar is stuck, it picks FRM tools on purpose, not all at once.

you need architecture

A toolbox is not enough; you need architecture. “Where do I start?” gets an answer when you know which pillar you are in and which gap comes next.

If your foresight always starts with scanning but never reaches acting, the problem is not tools; it is pillars. Six Pillars is a navigation map: not 39 random methods, but a path from question to probe. FRM is the library; Six Pillars is the compass.

methodsIndex.heroSub: All foresight, futures studies & strategic foresight methods — interactive workshops, lesson modules, and catalog entries in 7 categories.
methodsIndex.note: Each module includes founder intro, lesson plan, readings, and class assignment. New methods are added over time.

popper.heroSub: A framework to assess foresight quality — not prediction, but whether your exercise was truly creative, innovative, expert-driven, and interactive.

history.title: Foresight Diamond — quality assessment
history.origin: Rafael Popper developed the Foresight Diamond in EU FORLEARN projects to answer: how do we know a foresight exercise was good, not merely completed?
history.problem: FRM 3.0 lists methods but lacks an integrated quality metric to compare workshops, policy processes, and consulting deliverables.
history.applications: EU national foresight quality reviews, JRC workshop audits, and internal foresight team review before board presentations.

popper.intro: Each corner is a quality dimension. Weak foresight is often hollow in one or two corners — e.g. high Expertise but no real stakeholder Interaction.

pillars.title: Creativity
pillars.desc: Did unexpected ideas, fresh metaphors, and assumption-breaking occur? Without creativity, foresight is a trend report.

pillars.title: Innovation
pillars.desc: Did outputs surface new paths, policy options, or business models?

pillars.title: Expertise
pillars.desc: Was there deep domain knowledge, evidence, and analytical competence?

pillars.title: Interaction
pillars.desc: Were stakeholders genuinely engaged? Token attendance is not interaction.

popper.gap: Chapter 39 covers integration only; Popper's Diamond is a meta-tool applied across all other methods. Glenn & Gordon listed tools; Popper added a quality lens.
popper.case: A ministry ran Delphi — 200-page report. Popper audit: high Expertise, medium Interaction, low Creativity and Innovation. Result: redesign with CLA and scenarios — not a second Delphi.

hines.heroSub: Operational framework from Thinking About the Future: the full path from framing the question to action — for managers, not only researchers.

history.title: Six Pillars of Strategic Foresight
history.origin: At Houston's foresight program, Hines & Bishop treated FRM as a toolbox but managers needed a path. The Six Pillars answer "what do I do now?"
history.problem: FRM gives methods chapter by chapter; organizations don't know when to start scanning and when to finish planning.
history.applications: MBA foresight programs, organizational consulting, APF training, and executive courses in energy and health.

hines.intro: Each pillar is a measurable phase. Weakness in one breaks the chain — e.g. excellent forecasting without visioning yields shelf reports.

pillars.title: 1. Framing
pillars.desc: Define the question, system boundary, stakeholders, and "why foresight?". Without framing, everything is analyzed and nothing decided.

pillars.title: 2. Scanning
pillars.desc: Environmental scanning, weak signals, STEEP/PESTLE. FRM Ch.2 exists; Hines places it in a pillar sequence.

pillars.title: 3. Forecasting
pillars.desc: Trends, cross-impact, cone of plausibility. Exploring possibility space — not single-line prediction.

pillars.title: 4. Visioning
pillars.desc: Building preferred and normative futures. Bridge to backcasting and scenarios.

pillars.title: 5. Planning
pillars.desc: Turn insight into roadmaps, OKRs, policy. Where foresight dies or creates value.

pillars.title: 6. Acting
pillars.desc: Pilots, structural change, learning. Without acting, foresight is management decoration.

hines.gap: FRM has 39 separate methods; Six Pillars is an architecture. Hines & Bishop say: choose tools by pillar — not the reverse.
hines.case: After scanning, the team draws a plausibility cone: center = dominant trend, edges = wild cards. Visioning focuses on plausible layers — not every imaginable fantasy.

scenarios.heroSub: Beyond FRM Chapter 19: building 2–4 plausible futures for decisions under uncertainty — not single-line forecast.

history.institution: Royal Dutch Shell · Global Business Network
history.title: Scenario Planning as Strategic Decision Tool
history.origin: Shell under Wack showed scenarios can move from stories to strategic survival tools (1973 oil crisis). Schwartz popularized them for Silicon Valley and NGOs.
history.problem: FRM has Scenario, Toolbox, and Interactive chapters but Shell depth (critical uncertainties, 2×2, early indicators) cannot fit one chapter.
history.applications: Shell, South Africa post-apartheid scenarios, Singapore PMO, WEF, and energy/water planning in the Middle East.

scenarios.intro: Scenarios are not "correct prediction" — mental preparation for multiple futures.

pillars.title: 1. Identify focal decision
pillars.desc: What decision is this for? Without a decision, scenarios are entertainment.

pillars.title: 2. Critical uncertainties
pillars.desc: Which two variables matter most and are least certain?

pillars.title: 3. 2×2 matrix
pillars.desc: Four quadrants = four world logics. Narrative names (not A/B/C/D).

pillars.title: 4. Narrative & logic
pillars.desc: Each scenario must be internally consistent — a path from today exists.

pillars.title: 5. Early indicators
pillars.desc: What signals show which scenario is activating?

pillars.title: 6. Implications & options
pillars.desc: Under each scenario, what no-regret and what-if strategies?

pillars.title: 7. Organizational link
pillars.desc: Scenarios must connect to budgeting and risk — or they sit on shelves.

scenarios.gap: FRM Ch.19–21: Scenario, Toolbox, Interactive. Shell/Schwartz: a decision system with 2×2 and indicators. FRM mentions interactive scenarios; not Shell-style strategic survival.
scenarios.case: Axis 1: energy transition speed (slow/fast). Axis 2: geopolitics (open/closed). Four scenarios: "Regional green economy", "Fossil island", "Tech leap", "Chronic crisis". Each implies different power investment policy.

futurpreneurship.heroSub: From weak signal and scenario to venture: someone who sees the future, accepts risk, and builds.

history.title: Entrepreneurial Foresight / Futurpreneurship
history.origin: FRM 3.0 has Personal Futures (Ch.36) and Genius Forecasting — but no systematic bridge to startup and venture building. Futurpreneurship fills that gap: foresight → hypothesis → MVP → scale.
history.problem: Startups without foresight are blind; futurists without ventures only report.
history.applications: Accelerator design, corporate venture building, policy labs, and PhD foresight with practical outputs (this portal is an example).

futurpreneurship.intro: Ali Mansouri Stack: Scan → Scenario → Venture hypothesis → Prototype → Strategic pivot.

pillars.title: Sense
pillars.desc: Weak signals + CLA layers 1–2. What "unsaid" change is approaching?

pillars.title: Imagine
pillars.desc: Scenarios and short horizon vision. Which future is worth a venture?

pillars.title: Hypothesize
pillars.desc: Falsifiable business hypothesis: "If X by 2030, customer Y exists."

pillars.title: Build
pillars.desc: MVP, landing test, minimum-resource pilot — probing in Rohrbeck's terms.

pillars.title: Scale or Kill
pillars.desc: If signal confirms, scale; if not, pivot or kill fast — foresight prevents long wrong bets.

futurpreneurship.gap: FRM was written for policy and research labs; venture creation is out of scope. Futurpreneurship is what Silicon Valley does informally — with foresight rigor.
futurpreneurship.case: Signal: aging + AI tutor demand. Scenario: "Physical school boundary" fades. Venture: AI tutor platform with curriculum foresight — MVP in 90 days, not 5 years of research.

type2Fuzzy.heroSub: When even “how uncertain are we?” is disputed, a single 0.6 is not enough. IT2 spread tells you if consensus is real or just silent averaging.

history.founder: Lotfi A. Zadeh · Jerry M. Mendel · Robert I. John · Narendra Karnik
history.institution: UC Berkeley · USC · De Montfort · Nottingham
history.title: From fuzzy sets to FOU
history.origin: Zadeh (1965) said membership in a set is not only 0 or 1. Linguistic variables followed in 1975 (“hot”, “emerging”, “strong”). Mendel and Karnik at USC formalized Interval Type-2: each membership grade is a band. John at De Montfort and Nottingham extended GT2 and decision support.
history.problem: In scanning or Delphi workshops everyone gives a number and we average. Type-1 yields 0.6 and hides disagreement. IT2 can yield a similar center but FOU width tells you if consensus was real or silent averaging.
history.applications: Weak signals, scenario weights, expert synthesis, decisions under deep uncertainty, and a bridge to organizational probing (Rohrbeck FITness).

type2Fuzzy.intro: Not formulas — shared language for foresight, technical, and management teams.

pillars.title: Membership function (Type-1)
pillars.desc: Curve μ(x): how much x belongs to “emerging”. Simple and manager-friendly.

pillars.title: Footprint of Uncertainty (FOU)
pillars.desc: Region between upper and lower MFs. Wider = more expert or stakeholder spread.

pillars.title: Type-reduction (Karnik–Mendel)
pillars.desc: Collapse IT2 to Type-1 before defuzzification. Centroid is the common algorithm.

pillars.title: Linguistic variables
pillars.desc: “Weak”, “medium”, “strong” defined with fuzzy sets — same language as weak signals.

pillars.title: Fuzzy rule base
pillars.desc: IF signal strong AND consensus high THEN act fast. IT2 keeps uncertainty in rules.

pillars.title: General Type-2 (GT2)
pillars.desc: When the FOU itself is fuzzy. Heavier compute; for severe policy or tech ambiguity.

type2Fuzzy.gap: FRM has Decision Support but fuzzy logic is not a named method. Type-2 fits linguistic judgment under deep uncertainty where probability lacks a known distribution.
type2Fuzzy.case: Three experts: FOUs [0.35,0.72], [0.48,0.85], [0.22,0.55]. Type-1 mean ≈ 0.53. IT2 spread ≈ 0.36 → moderate consensus. The team runs a Delphi round or short probing instead of a crisp board report.

hubAdvanced.popperDesc: Assess foresight quality: Creativity, Innovation, Expertise, Interaction — a meta-tool over all methods.
hubAdvanced.hinesDesc: Framing → Scanning → Forecasting → Visioning → Planning → Acting — the manager's path.
hubAdvanced.scenariosDesc: Shell/Schwartz: 2×2, critical uncertainties, early indicators — beyond FRM's Scenario chapter.
hubAdvanced.futurDesc: Foresight + venture: from weak signal to MVP — what FRM 3.0 lacks.
hubAdvanced.type2Desc: Uncertainty about uncertainty — IT2 for weak signals, expert disagreement, and deep uncertainty.

type2Page.heroSub: For university teaching and corporate delivery: when even “how uncertain are we?” is disputed, a single 0.6 will not do. FOU shows real consensus vs silent averaging.
type2Page.theoryTitle: Why one membership grade is not enough
type2Page.theoryLead: Imagine a Delphi workshop: five experts score how “emerging” a trend is. You average to 0.6 and call it consensus. But if one expert said 0.9 and another 0.3, that 0.6 hides conflict — it is silent averaging, not agreement.
theoryNarrative: In 1965 Lotfi Zadeh argued the world is not only black and white. Membership in a set can be partial: tea can be “hot” without being like fire. Linguistic variables (“emerging”, “strong”) came next. Type-1 fuzzy logic assigns one number between 0 and 1 for each item.

In foresight everyone may use the same word but mean different numbers. One expert sees 0.7 from a technology angle; another 0.4 from the market. Type-1 averages to 0.55. The manager reads consensus; the disagreement stays hidden.

Mendel and Karnik at USC proposed treating the grade itself as uncertain — a band, not a point. Expert A: 0.4–0.7; Expert B: 0.2–0.5. The region across all bands is the Footprint of Uncertainty (FOU). Its width shows how far you are from real agreement.

Interval Type-2 (IT2) is the workhorse: lower and upper curves with FOU between them. IT2 is an honesty tool in foresight: center near 0.6 but spread 0.48 means you still lack consensus. IT2 does not replace probability; it fits linguistic judgment when you want disagreement visible.
type2Page.theoryExample: Three experts on EU AI regulation: bands [0.35,0.72], [0.48,0.85], [0.22,0.55]. Type-1 mean ≈ 0.53; IT2 spread ≈ 0.36 → moderate agreement at best. A second Delphi round or small probe beats a crisp board report.
foresightProse: IT2 helps when data are thin and opinions scatter — weak signals, Delphi panels. Keep each expert’s FOU and the aggregate FOU instead of silent averaging. Non-overlapping bands mean more probing, not a final verdict.

In scenarios and the cone of plausibility, “plausible” can be a band, not fake precision. Wide spread in Rohrbeck FITness means you have not probed yet; IT2 links to testing, not to claiming consensus.
type2Page.workflowProse: In a workshop: name one specific weak signal. Define a linguistic label (e.g. signal strength). Each expert gives lower and upper bounds. Read aggregate FOU — wide means disagreement, narrow means consensus. Decide light scanning, Delphi, or probing; write a one-page memo with mean, spread, and a 90-day recommendation.
type2Page.limitsProse: IT2 does not replace CLA, scenarios, or probability with a known distribution. Translate band width into decision language for non-technical managers. FOU is not a statistical confidence interval.
type2Page.theoryP1: In 1965 Lotfi Zadeh introduced fuzzy sets: membership is not only 0 or 1. In 1975 came linguistic variables such as “hot”, “emerging”, “strong”. Type-1 fuzzy logic formalized membership μ(x) in [0,1].
type2Page.theoryP2: In Type-1 each point x has one precise μ. That works when everyone shares roughly the same definition of “emerging”. In foresight the trouble usually starts here: experts still fight over the number itself.
type2Page.theoryP3: Jerry Mendel and Narendra Karnik at USC noticed real-world disagreement over μ itself. Expert A says 0.7, B says 0.4 — both can be right with different mental models. Type-2 treats membership as a band: the Footprint of Uncertainty (FOU).
type2Page.theoryP4: Interval Type-2 (IT2) is the workhorse: lower and upper membership functions. The region between them is the FOU. Karnik–Mendel type-reduction collapses the FOU to Type-1, but first you must see how wide disagreement is.
type2Page.theoryP5: In foresight IT2 is an honesty tool. Type-1 averaging 0.6 hides conflict. IT2 can yield a similar center but report spread = 0.48 — meaning you do not yet have real consensus.
type2Page.theoryP6: Robert John at De Montfort and Nottingham advanced General Type-2 (GT2) and decision support. GT2 when the FOU itself is fuzzy. For most foresight classes and workshops, IT2 is enough.
type2Page.theoryP7: A full fuzzy system (Mamdani or Takagi–Sugeno) has four stages: fuzzification, rule evaluation, aggregation, defuzzification. IT2 requires type-reduction before defuzzification. This module focuses on linguistic judgment and FOU, not industrial control.
type2Page.theoryP8: Key distinction: probability when you have (or assume) a distribution; IT2 when you have linguistic judgment and want disagreement explicit. IT2 does not replace probability; it complements it under deep uncertainty.

type2Page.pipelineTitle: Data path in a fuzzy system (Type-1 and IT2)
type2Page.pipelineIntro: Industrial control implements every stage. In foresight, fuzzification + FOU + conceptual type-reduction + memo is usually enough.

pipelineSteps.title: Fuzzification
pipelineSteps.desc: Crisp input (expert score) or linguistic label (“strong”) becomes a fuzzy set.

pipelineSteps.title: Rule evaluation
pipelineSteps.desc: IF signal strong AND consensus high THEN act fast. In IT2 antecedents and consequents are IT2.

pipelineSteps.title: Aggregation
pipelineSteps.desc: Rule outputs combine. In IT2 the result is a type-2 set.

pipelineSteps.title: Type-reduction
pipelineSteps.desc: IT2 → Type-1. Karnik–Mendel centroid is the common method.

pipelineSteps.title: Defuzzification
pipelineSteps.desc: Type-1 → crisp number y*. In foresight spread often matters more than y*.

type2Page.deepDiveTitle: What students should know (without formula overload)

deepDiveItems.title: Membership function
deepDiveItems.text: μ(x)∈[0,1] says how much x belongs to fuzzy set A. Common shapes: triangle, trapezoid, Gaussian. Trapezoids fit “emerging” intuitively in foresight.
deepDiveItems.note: Type-1: μ(x) is a number. IT2: μ(x) is a band [μ̲, μ̄].

deepDiveItems.title: Primary membership J(x,u)
deepDiveItems.text: In formal IT2, J(x,u) is secondary membership. For class: FOU = {(x,u) | u∈[μ̲(x), μ̄(x)]} is enough.
deepDiveItems.note: Exam tip: answer “what is FOU?” with a three-expert drawing.

deepDiveItems.title: Type-reduction (Karnik–Mendel)
deepDiveItems.text: Goal: collapse IT2 set to Type-1. Centroid integrates y·μ(y); in IT2 μ is an interval; KM iteratively finds y* and band [yL, yR].
deepDiveItems.note: In foresight: width of [yL,yR] ≈ disagreement spread.

deepDiveItems.title: Mamdani vs Takagi–Sugeno
deepDiveItems.text: Mamdani: fuzzy consequent. Takagi–Sugeno: crisp linear/constant consequent. TS dominates control; Mamdani is closer to human linguistic rules.
deepDiveItems.note: For weak-signal judgment, Mamdani-style rules are enough.

deepDiveItems.title: Epistemic vs aleatory uncertainty
deepDiveItems.text: Aleatory: inherent variability (coin toss). Epistemic: our ignorance (missing model). Expert disagreement is epistemic; IT2 targets that.
deepDiveItems.note: Knightian = severe epistemic without a known distribution.

type2Page.verdictTight: Low disagreement. Light scanning may suffice; a short probe still helps.
type2Page.verdictMedium: Moderate spread. Run a Delphi round or deep stakeholder interviews before deciding.
type2Page.verdictWide: Wide spread. No consensus yet; Rohrbeck-style probing beats a crisp report.

history.title: Corporate Foresight: Towards a Maturity Model
history.origin: In 2010, René Rohrbeck introduced the first structured model for measuring organizational foresight maturity in the academic book Corporate Foresight: Towards a Maturity Model. The work was based on multi-year field studies at large European companies at Aarhus University and later at EDHEC.
history.problem: Organizations could not measure their foresight maturity level, could not justify foresight investment, and foresight outputs were not connected to strategic decisions.
history.applications: Deployed at Siemens and Deutsche Telekom; benchmark studies showed foresight-driven organizations achieved 33% higher profitability and 200% greater market value growth than vulnerable competitors.

hero.title: Organizational Foresight Maturity Model & Future FITness
hero.subtitle: How organizations move beyond static Future Readiness to dynamic Future FITness — continuous organizational fitness and adaptation to shape tomorrow's markets.

readiness.title: What Is Future Readiness?
readiness.sub: Forecasting trends, understanding scenarios, and strategic planning to manage uncertainty.
readiness.p1: Future Readiness means the ability to anticipate change, understand it deeply, and plan purposeful responses. Rooted in uncertainty management: scanning risks and turning them into major opportunities.
readiness.p2: Today's world faces rapid technological change — from generative AI to climate disruption. Organizations and individuals prepared for the future see these shifts as opportunities for leadership and progress, not paralyzing threats.
readiness.challengeText: Is your organization — or you personally — ready for fundamental future changes? If you wanted to make a change today that builds future fitness, what would your first key step be?
readiness.foresightText: Foresight means deeply understanding trends and analyzing alternative scenarios. Companies that had piloted remote-work technology before the COVID pandemic adapted to lockdown conditions far faster.
readiness.agilityText: Agility means fast decision-making and rapid pivoting in response to unexpected conditions. Zoom expanded server infrastructure within weeks during COVID to serve hundreds of millions of new users.
readiness.sustainabilityTitle: The Critical Role of Sustainability in Portfolio Strategy
readiness.sustainabilityP1: Sustainability is more than social responsibility — it is smart business strategy. Aligning with ESG goals reduces climate risk and creates lasting competitive advantage in global markets.
readiness.sustainabilityP2: IKEA committed to 100% recycled and renewable materials by 2030, building customer loyalty and lowering long-term material costs. Tesla (electric vehicles) and Amazon (advanced logistics) secured survival and leadership by investing in long-term trends.

apps.label: Education (Finland)
apps.text: Finland's project-based, innovation-focused education system prepares students to solve complex real-world problems of tomorrow.

apps.label: Policy (Singapore)
apps.text: Singapore's Smart Nation program uses technology to prepare the economy and citizens' quality of life for the aging challenge.

apps.label: Healthcare (NHS UK)
apps.text: Big data analytics and AI for early disease diagnosis and hospital resource optimization.

apps.label: Transport & Crisis (Netherlands & Japan)
apps.text: The Netherlands with green transport and Japan with earthquake warning and crisis management systems are national resilience leaders.

readinessCards.title: Trend Forecasting & Analysis
readinessCards.desc: Analyzing key external trends such as demographic shifts or emerging technologies and their industry impact.

readinessCards.title: Rapid Adaptation to Change
readinessCards.desc: High agility: extraordinarily fast decision-making and strategic pivoting when facing crises.

readinessCards.title: Creativity & Innovation Development
readinessCards.desc: Creating new solutions for present and future challenges through process changes or novel products.

readinessCards.title: Continuous Learning Culture
readinessCards.desc: Ongoing upskilling and team learning to keep pace with global scientific and technological change.

readinessCards.title: Smart Technology Use
readinessCards.desc: Deploying powerful tools like AI and blockchain for better forecasting, planning, and process execution.

fitness.title: What Is Future FITness?
fitness.sub: A theoretical comparison of continuous adaptation and optimization versus traditional strategic readiness.
fitness.p1: While Future Readiness focuses on forecasting and preparation, Future FITness goes further: the continuous, dynamic ability to maintain optimal performance, adapt constantly, grow, and redefine structures when facing unexpected challenges.
fitness.p2: Professor René Rohrbeck's long-term studies at Aarhus University showed that "vigilant and future-fit" companies averaged 33% higher profitability and 200% greater market value growth than vulnerable competitors.
fitness.teeceTitle: Alignment with David Teece's Dynamic Capabilities

teece.title: Sensing
teece.text: Continuous identification of change signals, trends, and threats in highly turbulent industry environments (aligned with Kathleen Eisenhardt's theory).

teece.title: Seizing
teece.text: Capturing new opportunities through agile business model design and investment channels aligned with plausible futures.

teece.title: Transforming
teece.text: Continuous renewal of tangible and intangible organizational assets to maintain competitive advantage and environmental alignment.

fitness.dashboardP1: This dashboard shows the interactive structure of organizational dynamic capabilities. Organizations can assess technological alignment and implement Perceiving, Prospecting, and Probing processes in balanced form.
fitness.dashboardP2: The match between industry turbulence and foresight maturity determines survival and profitability — preventing structural rigidity collapses like Nokia and Kodak.
fitness.stepsTitle: 5-Step Systematic Process to Achieve Future FITness

fitnessSteps.title: Assess Current State
fitnessSteps.desc: Identify internal strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats using a SWOT matrix.

fitnessSteps.title: Define Future Vision
fitnessSteps.desc: Define desirable futures and set goals based on global trends as a roadmap.

fitnessSteps.title: Develop Capabilities
fitnessSteps.desc: Invest in R&D (like Apple) to strengthen innovation capacity.

fitnessSteps.title: Implement & Experiment
fitnessSteps.desc: Test ideas with high flexibility (like Tesla experimenting with different electric vehicle models).

fitnessSteps.title: Monitor & Continuously Improve
fitnessSteps.desc: Continuously track results from feedback and optimize models (like IKEA on sustainability goals).

archetypes.title: 4 Foresight Maturity Archetypes in Industry
archetypes.sub: Organizations are classified into four groups based on how well "foresight thinking maturity" matches "industry change speed and turbulence."

vigilant.title: Vigilant
vigilant.desc: Organizational foresight matches or exceeds industry change speed. Continuous weak-signal monitoring and rapid response.
vigilant.note: 33% higher profitability and 200% greater market value growth

detail.title: Vigilant Archetype
detail.alignment: High industry turbulence + high strategic foresight maturity (full alignment with transformation)
detail.symptoms: Leading organizations with high dynamic capabilities. Per David Teece, they combine continuous Sensing with fast Seizing and ongoing Transforming. Immune to analysis paralysis; they creatively destroy their own product portfolios before competitors do.
detail.reps: Nvidia (CUDA platform), Netflix (DVD to streaming and original content).

neurotic.title: Neurotic
neurotic.desc: High foresight capacity but in a slow-changing industry. Leads to over-analysis and false alarm.

detail.title: Neurotic Archetype
detail.alignment: Low industry turbulence + excessive foresight maturity (imbalance from hyper-sensitivity)
detail.symptoms: Over-analysis and decision paralysis. Despite massive investment in technology observatories, they cannot separate signal from noise. Fear-driven culture produces hundreds of parallel scenarios with no operational output, blocking strategic decisions.
detail.reps: Traditional oil & gas majors that invested billions in unrelated renewables before market demand materialized.

vulnerable.title: Vulnerable
vulnerable.desc: Formal strategic processes but disconnected from change signals. Repeated surprise by disruptive competitor innovations.

detail.title: Vulnerable Archetype
detail.alignment: High industry turbulence + low foresight maturity (strategic danger zone)
detail.symptoms: Organizations in turbulent industries without scanning capability. Reliant on linear 5-year planning, interpreting rapid tech change as temporary fluctuations. Cognitive rigidity; they react when market value is destroyed and recovery costs are crippling.
detail.reps: Kodak (ignoring digital cameras), Nokia (missing the software touchscreen revolution).

danger.title: In Danger
danger.desc: No scanning system in a highly turbulent industry. Facing dissolution or total market share loss.

detail.title: In Danger Archetype
detail.alignment: Low industry turbulence + low foresight maturity (hidden stagnation and decline)
detail.symptoms: The silent death archetype — organizations in apparently stable industries with no environmental monitoring. Trapped in long-term stability illusion. The smallest environmental shock (regulatory change or sudden substitute technology) can destroy the business model in seconds.
detail.reps: Traditional taxi unions before Uber/Snapp; physical travel agencies before online booking.

roles.title: 3 Critical Foresight Roles in Innovation Dynamics
roles.sub: René Rohrbeck proves foresight must play three roles — initiator, strategist, and opponent — in the innovation value chain.

initiator.title: 1. Initiator — Spark of Innovation
initiator.desc: The Initiator operates in the Perceiving phase: continuously scanning the external environment, discovering emerging trends, and converting them into initial innovation concepts.
duties: Launch and maintain technology radars and emerging trend monitoring.
Identify weak signals of change before they become mainstream.
Run creative brainstorming workshops to generate novel innovation concepts.
initiator.example: Deutsche Telekom's foresight unit, monitoring miniature sensors, sparked IoT projects by proposing connecting farmland to the network.

strategist.title: 2. Strategist — Investment Portfolio Guide
strategist.desc: The Strategist operates in the Prospecting phase: steering innovation investment portfolios based on future scenarios and organizational strategy.
duties: Develop offensive and defensive foresight scenarios for the industry.
Draw technology roadmaps to align departments.
Define shared future visions for the organization.
strategist.example: Nvidia leadership analyzed data-processing scenarios and redirected the entire R&D portfolio toward AI data-center architecture.

opponent.title: 3. Opponent — Devil's Advocate
opponent.desc: The Opponent operates in the Probing phase: challenging rigid assumptions, critically evaluating projects, and preventing management confirmation bias.
duties: Run harsh scenarios to test project survival and resilience.
Conduct war-gaming to expose internal weaknesses.
Facilitate creative cannibalization to retire profitable legacy products.
opponent.example: When Steve Jobs launched the iPhone, he opposed the iPod (a top seller) — knowing a competitor would integrate music players into phones if Apple didn't.

cases.title: Responding to Change with Three-Dimensional Dynamic Capability DNA
cases.sub: Systematic analysis of failures and successes through Sensing, Seizing, and Transforming.

netflix.title: Netflix — Leader in Dynamic Capability Reinvention
netflix.scanning: In the late 1990s, leadership monitored home internet bandwidth and video compression, recognizing physical DVD obsolescence. This was precise weak-signal sensing.
netflix.seizing: In 2007, launched online streaming. In 2012, allocated major resources to original content (House of Cards) to reduce studio dependency.
netflix.transforming: Fully shifted from internal data centers to cloud (AWS) and continuously redefined around generative AI and deep personalization. Lesson: readiness to destroy current successful revenue models.

nvidia.title: Nvidia — Risky Bet on a Future Computing Paradigm
nvidia.scanning: In the early 2000s, Jensen Huang saw parallel GPU architecture had applications beyond game rendering — transformative for scientific computing and AI algorithms.
nvidia.seizing: In 2006, heavily invested in developing the CUDA platform. Wall Street repeatedly criticized the project with no market demand, but Nvidia persisted.
nvidia.transforming: With deep learning in 2012, Nvidia became the world's ready AI hardware-software hub. Huang transformed the company from a graphics card maker to an AI infrastructure giant. Lesson: persistence on long-term differentiating capabilities.

nokia.title: Nokia — Structural Rigidity & Success Trap
nokia.scanning: Nokia received signals about touchscreens and software ecosystems; they even developed touchscreen tablet prototypes years before the iPhone.
nokia.seizing: Due to the success trap and high button-phone margins, executives kept Symbian. Bureaucracy prevented danger signals from becoming bold decisions.
nokia.transforming: Fear culture and optimistic reporting hid technical weaknesses. Rigid bureaucracy blocked reinvention; Symbian couldn't compete with iOS and Android. Nokia lost the mobile market entirely. Lesson: cognitive rigidity can neutralize the best scanning capacity.

blockbuster.title: Blockbuster — Foresight Sacrificed to Short-Term Shareholders
blockbuster.scanning: Blockbuster managers tracked Netflix's growth and digital distribution, knowing the physical rental model was ending.
blockbuster.seizing: CEO John Antioco launched an online platform and eliminated late fees — actions aimed at capturing streaming opportunities.
blockbuster.transforming: Short-term-minded boards fired the CEO due to temporary revenue drops (late fees were 16% of profit), restored fees, and refocused on physical stores — leading to bankruptcy. Lesson: short-term shareholder interests vs. long-term survival investment is the biggest transformation barrier.

formula.title: The Three-Part Future FITness Formula
formula.sub: This model connects foresight (F) to the innovation engine (I) and guarantees transformation (T) as the final outcome.
formula.clickPrompt: Click any letter above to view details.

foresight.title: Continuous Scanning & Interpretation of Change Signals
foresight.body: Foresight is the organization's systematic ability to monitor the external environment, discover weak signals, and understand trends. This corresponds to Perceiving and Prospecting in Rohrbeck's theory.
items: Continuous monitoring of macro economic, technological, and political environments.
Analyzing plausible scenarios and developing roadmaps.
Preventing strategic surprise from sudden disruptions.

innovation.title: Converting Insights into Novel Products & Tools
innovation.body: Innovation is the execution engine of foresight. Ideas become rapid prototypes — corresponding to Probing and requiring Initiator, Strategist, and Opponent roles.
items: Challenging current assumptions through the Opponent role.
Launching pilot projects to validate market assumptions.

transformation.title: Structural & Cultural Redesign for Long-Term Survival
transformation.body: Transformation is the ultimate goal. Foresight and innovation must change culture, structure, and resource allocation — building new organizational DNA for long-term survival.
items: Reforming formal structure and reducing bureaucracy for greater agility.
Embracing continuous learning and reducing fear of failure among staff.
Continuously aligning dynamic capabilities with emerging technological trends.

rohrbeckDims.title: Five Dimensions of Organizational Foresight Maturity
rohrbeckDims.sub: Based on Professor René Rohrbeck's longitudinal research, strategic foresight capability is measured and calibrated across five structural dimensions.

dims.title: Information Sources & Quality
dims.desc: Breadth, depth, and quality of environmental scanning; bandwidth of monitored information, scanning beyond current industry boundaries, and early weak-signal discovery.

dims.title: Foresight Methodologies
dims.desc: Diversity and effectiveness of tools; regular use of qualitative and quantitative methods (scenario planning, Delphi, technology roadmapping, cross-impact analysis).

dims.title: Human Capital & Experts
dims.desc: Competence, futures literacy, and expert mix; manager training, intelligence networks, staff motivation to share insights, and recruiting foresight talent.

dims.title: Formal Structure & Decision-Making
dims.desc: Formal feedback mechanisms and bureaucracy; connecting foresight outputs to strategic planning, R&D budgeting, and administrative flexibility.

dims.title: Uncertainty Acceptance & Innovation
dims.desc: Foundational values; tolerance of failure, encouraging creative and non-linear thinking, willingness to creatively cannibalize before competitors, and no fear of voicing change warnings.

assessment.title: Organizational Foresight Maturity Index
assessment.sub: Based on Rohrbeck's five maturity dimensions, assess your organization's future fitness and receive strategic analysis.

info.label: 1. Information & Scanning
info.desc: Access, monitoring, and analysis of change signals and major external trends in the organization.

methods.label: 2. Methods & Tools
methods.desc: Regular use of scientific foresight methods such as scenario writing, Delphi, and technology radar.

people.label: 3. People & Expertise
people.desc: Staff skills training, external expert engagement, and organizational participation in futures thinking.

org.label: 4. Formal Organization
org.desc: Dedicated foresight structures, connecting outputs to board decisions, and independent budgeting.

culture.label: 5. Organizational Culture
culture.desc: Change acceptance, no fear of failure, and intrapreneurship with senior management support.

fit.description: Congratulations! Your organization has exceptional dynamic capabilities. You don't just predict change — you design innovative business models that redefine your industry's future. Keep focusing on open innovation and ecosystem participation.
recs: Lead the industry by sharing knowledge and participating in startup ecosystems.
Invest in high-risk disruptive technologies (quantum and bio-computing).
Balance optimizing current processes with developing entirely new markets.

prepared.description: Your organization performs well in trend scanning and readiness, but still needs stronger Probing processes and cultural transformation. Don't just wait for the future — actively create the desired future.
recs: Increase rapid prototyping projects to test technology assumptions.
Delegate more authority to frontline staff for faster decision-making.
Build a shared foresight language across organizational departments.

vulnerable.description: Warning! Your organization is in the danger zone. Over-reliance on traditional processes and cultural resistance to change threatens survival in market uncertainty. Urgent transformation in information infrastructure and management mindset is required.
recs: Immediately launch monthly environmental scanning to identify emerging threats.
Run foresight thinking and crisis management workshops for leaders.
Reduce formal bureaucracy to enable rapid market response.

foresightCards.title: Rapid Adaptation Index
foresightCards.desc: Agility Index: speed and effectiveness of product portfolio pivots (like fast product cycles in IT companies).

foresightCards.title: Continuous Innovation Rate
foresightCards.desc: Innovation Rate: number and quality of alternative solutions and market leadership (like Tesla's annual innovation releases).

foresightCards.title: Organizational Resilience
foresightCards.desc: Resilience Score: ability to recover quickly after financial and environmental disruptions (like Japan).

foresightCards.title: Ecosystem Collaboration
foresightCards.desc: Collaboration Score: joint value creation with partners and supply chain stakeholders (like Amazon).

foresightCards.title: Organizational Learning
foresightCards.desc: L&D Metrics: hours of specialized training and workforce skill upgrades per year (like Google).

radar.title: Emerging Technology Radar & Strategic Monitoring
radar.sub: Interactive technology radar simulation to prevent strategic surprise. Click radar dots to load impact analysis and recommendations.
radar.defaultText: A strategic tool to prevent surprise in telecommunications and IT. Click the pulsing dots to view details.
radar.dtCaseTitle: Why Was Deutsche Telekom Selected as Benchmark?
radar.dtCaseBody: Selecting Deutsche Telekom is scientifically grounded. A major part of René Rohrbeck's doctoral work and reference book on the foresight maturity model was built directly on multi-year operational study at this telecom giant. It is a successful example of technology radar integration connected to strategic processes and risk reduction.
radar.dtChallengesBody:
Resistance to change: strengthen open culture and change acceptance.
Skills gap: continuous reskilling programs.
Open ecosystem complexity: deploy open, transparent technologies.
Short/long-term goal conflict: separate budgeting and three-horizon innovation model.

genai.title: Generative AI
genai.desc: Large language models and image generation models for automated content, code, and workflow optimization.
genai.impact: Routine task automation, up to 40% production efficiency gains, and fully personalized user experiences.

agentic.title: Agentic AI
agentic.desc: Advanced AI systems that receive high-level goals and autonomously design, test, and execute multi-step tasks.
agentic.impact: Reduced direct human oversight, independent supply chain conflict resolution, and real-time decision processes.

nlp.title: Advanced NLP
nlp.desc: Natural language understanding for speech, text, sentiment analysis, and hidden knowledge extraction from documents.
nlp.impact: True 24/7 support via smart voice assistants and early customer dissatisfaction detection on social media.

6g.title: 6G Mobile Communications
6g.desc: Terabit-per-second speeds, microsecond latency, and integration of terrestrial networks with satellites for full global coverage.
6g.impact: Foundation for internet of senses, remote collaborative robots, and 3D holographic communications.

iot.title: IoT Ecosystems
iot.desc: Connecting hundreds of millions of smart sensors for data collection from operations, warehouses, and logistics.
iot.impact: Energy waste reduction, smart agricultural process monitoring, and improved factory equipment productivity.

quantum.title: Quantum Computing
quantum.desc: Using quantum qubits for physical simulations and complex mathematical optimizations beyond classical computers.
quantum.impact: Faster drug discovery, advanced portfolio optimization, and serious risks to current cybersecurity and encryption.

edge.title: Edge Computing
edge.desc: Processing data at the source (sensor or camera) instead of sending to central cloud servers to reduce latency and bandwidth.
edge.impact: Instant autonomous vehicle navigation and fast security camera image analysis without network overhead.

biocomputing.title: Bio-computing
biocomputing.desc: Engineering DNA and biological cells for logical computation and data storage with high durability and near-zero energy consumption.
biocomputing.impact: Replacing massive data centers with millimeter-scale, eco-friendly big data storage systems.

metaverse.title: Metaverse & Spatial Computing
metaverse.desc: Integrating VR, AR, and interactive 3D spaces for digital collaboration and professional simulators.
metaverse.impact: Major shift in remote education, surgical simulation, and organizational digital twin creation.

history.title: Causal Layered Analysis (CLA)
history.origin: Sohail Inayatullah developed CLA in the late 1990s and published it widely through the 2000s via articles, books, and Metafuture workshops. The method moves foresight from surface events toward discourse deconstruction and new metaphor creation.
history.problem: Traditional foresight often stops at headlines, statistics, and surface trends (Litany) — missing structural, discursive, and mythic roots.
history.applications: Singapore Prime Minister's Office (PMO), UNESCO, Taiwan government, and Metafuture workshops worldwide.

claPage.heroSub: A deep foresight method for breaking surface problems apart, exploring discursive and civilizational roots, and creating alternative metaphors for transformative scenarios.
claPage.theoryLead: CLA starts where surface news stops: when you ask “why is this an issue at all?” and the answer is more than headlines and stats.
theoryNarrative: Sohail Inayatullah built CLA on post-structural thought — especially Michel Foucault's genealogy. Foucault showed that “truth” is shaped by discourse and power; CLA deconstructs issues layer by layer to reveal hidden assumptions.

Moving from Litany (daily news) to systemic causes, then worldview, and finally myth helps stakeholders escape imposed scenarios. The goal is transformative alternative metaphors — not only describing the present.

In practice: see what media say (layer 1), which policies and structures produce that story (layer 2), which beliefs legitimise it (layer 3), and which myth or metaphor holds collective imagination (layer 4). Each layer is deeper, harder to change — and more durable when it does change.
claPage.theoryP1: CLA, created by Sohail Inayatullah, draws on post-structural thought — especially Michel Foucault's genealogy. Foucault showed that "truth" is shaped by discourse and power; CLA deconstructs issues layer by layer to reveal hidden assumptions.
claPage.theoryP2: Moving from Litany (daily news) to systemic causes, then worldview, and finally myth helps stakeholders escape imposed scenarios and build transformative alternative metaphors to reimagine present and future.

litany.title: The most visible level — daily news and statistics
litany.body: Issues, trends, and numbers seen in media every day. Information is scattered, often emotional, and lacks historical context. Public reaction tends toward passivity or blame. Power discourses use this layer to steer attention.
litany.question: What do media and public opinion say about this problem?
litany.language: Journalism, raw statistics, emotional and reactive tone

systemic.title: Technical, economic, historical, and political causes
systemic.body: Structures and policies that produce the Litany layer: laws, institutions, markets, bureaucracy, and feedback loops. Experts and policymakers work here, but deeper assumptions remain unchallenged.
systemic.question: Which policies, institutions, and structures created this issue?

worldview.title: Mental frameworks and legitimizing discourses
worldview.body: Beliefs and paradigms that legitimize Layer 2 structures — from neoliberalism and positivism to traditionalism and environmentalism. Here the boundaries of "truth" and "natural order" are exposed — Foucault's discursive power at work.
worldview.question: Which beliefs, ideologies, and discourses support these structures?
worldview.language: Philosophical, cultural, discourse analysis, deep theory

myth.title: Collective unconscious, archetypes, and metaphors
myth.body: The deepest layer: stories, emotions, and metaphors that keep worldviews alive across centuries. Change here is hard, but lasting transformation requires new metaphors.
myth.question: Which archetype, story, or metaphor dominates our collective unconscious?

singapore.title: Case study: Singapore waste management & smart transit
singapore.intro: In the early 2010s, Singapore's PMO Futures Office used CLA to deconstruct the cultural roots of waste buildup and metro overcrowding:
singapore.l1Text: Metro delays, garbage bags piling in apartments, citizen complaints in media.
singapore.l2Text: Insufficient budget for more train cars, landfill nearing capacity, weak recycling rules.
singapore.l3Text: "Singapore = luxury international hotel" — citizens as demanding customers, government as pure service provider.
singapore.l4Text: Hidden metaphor of "temporary hotel guests" — no emotional ownership of shared cleanliness.
singapore.lesson: Transformative metaphor: Reframing to "Singapore = shared family garden home" and a "collaborative sustainability" discourse revived ownership and resolved systemic deadlocks.

cases.sub: Explore major issues in themed bundles to reveal the discourses and myths behind each domain.

workspace.litanyPlaceholder: What headlines or stats repeat every day?

israel.title: Iran & Israel
israel.l1:
• Headlines: missile defense alerts, UN statements, travel warnings
  • Oil and currency swings, naval drills in Hormuz
  • Public reaction: war anxiety, panic buying
israel.l2:
• Arms imbalance and "mutual deterrence"
  • Abraham Accords/NATO vs. resistance axis
  • Cyber war, layered air defense, great-power interests
israel.l3:
• Discourse of anti-hegemony and liberation of Quds
  • vs. existential Zionist security discourse
  • Clash of Western liberal order and multipolar Eurasia
israel.l4:
• Iranian myth: Ashura, Fereydun vs. Zahhak
  • Israeli myth: Holocaust, Masada complex
  • Alternative: from "minefield" to "shared oasis ecosystem"

usa.title: Iran & USA
usa.l1:
• New OFAC sanctions, mutual diplomatic statements
  • Tanker seizures, blocked foreign assets
  • Rial drop, inflation, fear of trade isolation
usa.l2:
• Dollar hegemony and SWIFT control
  • No formal diplomacy since 1979
  • Congress secondary sanctions, Hormuz strategic role
usa.l3:
• American exceptionalism and liberal order
  • vs. independence and anti-hegemony discourse
  • Conflicting definitions of responsibility vs. sovereignty
usa.l4:
• US myth: lone sheriff against outlaws
  • Iranian myth: Kaveh the blacksmith vs. Zahhak
  • Alternative: from "Rome vs. Sassanid war" to "digital Silk Road bridge"

water.title: Water crisis in Iran
water.l1:
• Drying lakes (Urmia, Hamun, Bakhtegan)
  • Land subsidence, water-rights conflicts, dust storms
  • Reaction: blame government, climate despair
water.l2:
• Food self-sufficiency in dry plains
  • Dam building without EIA, illegal wells
  • Low water tariffs, irrigation efficiency under 35%
water.l3:
• "Engineer nature" development worldview
  • vs. ecological sustainability and local wisdom
  • Conflicting definitions of progress
water.l4:
• Modern myth: conquering engineer with concrete dams
  • Ancient myth: Anahita and Tishtar vs. Apush
  • Alternative: from "bleeding the land dry" to "harmony with desert & qanat"

future-iran.title: Iran's core future challenge
future-iran.l1:
• Currency and housing spikes, stock market falls, capital flight
  • 40%+ inflation, power and gas cuts
  • Filtering, slow internet, mass emigration
future-iran.l2:
• Rentier oil economy and quasi-state monopolies
  • FATF non-compliance, heavy bureaucracy
  • Pension fund deficits, chronic budget gaps
future-iran.l3:
• "Resistive economy & inward turn" discourse
  • vs. "developmentalist global integration"
  • Conflict: sovereignty priority vs. growth
future-iran.l4:
• Phoenix myth: guaranteed survival after crisis
  • Nostalgia for "great imperial civilization"
  • Alternative: "Persian carpet" — diverse threads in one coherent pattern

brain-drain.title: Brain drain & talent exodus
brain-drain.l1:
• Migration of thousands of doctors, IT engineers, top graduates
  • Embassy queues, hospital staff shortages
  • Reaction: regret, normalized emigration
brain-drain.l2:
• Economic instability, falling purchasing power
  • Low pay vs. Gulf neighbors
  • Non-merit appointments, filtering, weak research infrastructure
brain-drain.l3:
• "Sacred national service & loyalty" discourse
  • vs. "right to grow and join global science"
  • Conflict: duty vs. meritocracy
brain-drain.l4:
• "Unwanted child" archetype
  • Alternative: Brain Drain → Brain Circulation — transnational science networks

generation-gap.title: Deep generational gap
generation-gap.l1:
• Different dress, music, digital lifestyles
  • Declining marriage and birth rates, state media losing influence
  • Reaction: parental confusion, relational disconnect
generation-gap.l2:
• Mobile internet and Gen Z global culture
  • Rigid formal education, broadcast media monopoly
  • Institutions out of sync with networked reality
generation-gap.l3:
• Patriarchal, collectivist traditional worldview
  • vs. individual rights, digital global identity
  • Conflict: family authority vs. autonomy
generation-gap.l4:
• "Two strangers under one roof" myth
  • Alternative: "Intergenerational bridge" — warp of old wisdom, weft of new creativity

brain-drain.title: Brain drain & talent exodus
brain-drain.litany: Headlines on elite migration, doctor and pilot shortages, long embassy queues.
brain-drain.systemic: Economic instability, low pay, non-merit hiring, lack of modern lab facilities.
brain-drain.worldview: "National/religious duty" vs. "individual growth & borderless global science market."
brain-drain.myth: "Unwanted child" myth — alternative: "brain circulation" and transnational science networks.

inflation.title: Inflation & severe livelihood decline
inflation.litany: Instant dollar/gold swings, shrinking household budgets, rent spikes, record inflation.
inflation.systemic: Money supply expansion, budget deficits, SWIFT isolation, blocked oil revenues.
inflation.worldview: "Centralized justice & command economy" vs. "free market & monetary reform."
inflation.myth: Sisyphus myth — alternative: "fiscal discipline & productive domestic economy."

generation-gap.title: Deep intergenerational values gap
generation-gap.litany: Youth dress clashes with norms, different music tastes, parents' digital loneliness complaints.
generation-gap.systemic: Fast internet, social networks as reference, ineffective formal education.
generation-gap.worldview: "Preserve traditional ethics & family" vs. "freedom of choice & Gen Z digital identity."
generation-gap.myth: "Invisible wall at home" — alternative: "intergenerational synergy bridge."

history.title: Origins of the Futures Wheel
history.origin: Jerome C. Glenn developed the Futures Wheel in 1971–1972 at the AC/UNU Millennium Project to map direct and indirect consequence chains from a central event. The phenomenon sits at the hub and experts draw 1st, 2nd, and 3rd order branches radially.
history.problem: Linear analysis examines a single event, but secondary, feedback, and long-range effects — often the decisive ones — stay hidden.
history.applications: Boeing's pilotless aircraft assessment, WHO and US DoD policy workshops, and Millennium Project hydropolitical crisis research.

wheelPage.heroSub: Sequential causal consequence mapping — tracing 1st, 2nd, and 3rd order effects of Iran's climate crisis.

boeing.title: Case Study: Boeing's Pilotless Passenger Aircraft Assessment
boeing.intro: Boeing used a Structured Futures Wheel to assess fully autonomous commercial aircraft through 2045:

cards.title: 1st Order (Direct Effects)
cards.body: Eliminating pilot human error, reducing crew costs, and optimizing fuel via real-time flight algorithms.

cards.title: 2nd Order (Secondary Effects)
cards.body: Lower ticket prices, pilot layoffs, union resistance, and new cyber-attack threats to aircraft.

cards.title: 3rd Order (Long-Term Effects)
cards.body: Complex liability law challenges and passengers' psychological resistance to trusting machines with their lives.

boeing.lesson: Strategic lesson: the biggest challenge was not technology but societal psychology and legal liability (3rd order); investment shifted toward adaptive semi-autonomous systems.

wheelPage.boeingTitle: Case Study: Boeing's Pilotless Passenger Aircraft Assessment
wheelPage.boeingIntro: Boeing used a Structured Futures Wheel to assess fully autonomous commercial aircraft through 2045:
wheelPage.boeingO1Text: Eliminating pilot human error, reducing crew costs, and optimizing fuel via real-time flight algorithms.
wheelPage.boeingO2Text: Lower ticket prices, pilot layoffs, union resistance, and new cyber-attack threats to aircraft.
wheelPage.boeingO3Text: Complex liability law challenges and passengers' psychological resistance to trusting machines with their lives.
wheelPage.boeingLesson: Strategic lesson: the biggest challenge was not technology but societal psychology and legal liability (3rd order); investment shifted toward adaptive semi-autonomous systems.
wheelPage.diagramP1: Stepwise structure of 1st order (drought, dust storms, heat waves), 2nd order (aquifer depletion, farmer livelihoods, migration), and 3rd order national-security effects.
wheelPage.diagramP2: Systematic analysis reveals feedback loops, cross-effects, and border defense strain — enabling national resilience solutions.
wheelPage.modelsSub: Beyond a simple diagram — four key modeling approaches in advanced projects.
wheelPage.processTitle: 11 Steps to Draw & Run a Futures Wheel

steps.title: Define the Central Issue
steps.desc: Clearly state the origin event at the hub center and set temporal/spatial boundaries.

steps.title: Assemble Expert Panel (Delphi)
steps.desc: Recruit interdisciplinary experts to reduce bias and groupthink.

steps.title: Identify 1st-Order Consequences
steps.desc: Brainstorm: "What immediate technical, environmental, or social effects occur?"

steps.title: Screen & Select Consequences
steps.desc: Filter by correlation and impact strength; remove redundancy.

steps.title: Extract 2nd-Order Consequences
steps.desc: For each 1st-order node: "What is the next secondary reaction?"

steps.title: Extract 3rd-Order Consequences
steps.desc: Trace chains to long-range security, identity, and systemic collapse effects.

steps.title: Map Network & Cross-Links
steps.desc: Discover feedback loops and amplifying cross-branch relationships.

steps.title: Quantify & Cross-Analyze
steps.desc: Estimate probability and impact to identify highest-risk paths.

steps.title: Policy & Response Design
steps.desc: Attach Policy Response Nodes to negative outcomes for an action roadmap.

steps.title: Delphi Validation Round
steps.desc: Independent expert review to reduce availability heuristic bias.

steps.title: Continuous Monitoring
steps.desc: Define KPIs and periodically refresh the wheel with new weak signals.

classic.title: Classic Futures Wheel (Jerome Glenn Model)
classic.desc: Introduced in 1971 by Jerome C. Glenn (Millennium Project), this model relies on creative brainstorming and free exploration without preset categories. The central phenomenon sits at the hub and experts draw radial consequence chains.
classic.focus: Creative brainstorming and open, multi-directional mapping of direct and indirect qualitative consequences.
classic.tool: Live workshops or digital platforms (Miro/Mural) with connected color-coded cards.
classic.output: US DoD / GE assessment of internet and IT impacts on US Army operations in the late 1970s.

structured.title: Structured STEEP Futures Wheel
structured.desc: To prevent one-dimensional analysis, the wheel is segmented by STEEP variables (Social, Technological, Economic, Environmental, Political). Each consequence is placed in its sector and grows secondary and tertiary branches.
structured.focus: Systematic classification for cross-analysis of macro environmental and technological trends.
structured.tool: Divide the canvas into 5–6 STEEP sectors and run focused brainstorm panels per sector.
structured.output: WHO study mapping pandemic and lifestyle change consequences through 2040.

quantitative.title: Quantitative Futures Wheel
quantitative.desc: In rigorous foresight projects, the wheel integrates probability and impact scoring. Experts rate each node for probability (0–1) and impact/desirability (−10 to +10) to identify critical risk paths.
quantitative.focus: Numerical estimation, mathematical prioritization of threats, and strategic path valuation.
quantitative.tool: Probabilistic Delphi surveys, statistical simulation, and risk matrix algorithms.
quantitative.output: Millennium Project research on MENA hydropolitical crises and water-scarcity security collapse paths.

action.title: Policy-Oriented Futures Wheel
action.desc: Rather than merely describing the future, this model drives action. At the end of negative chains, experts attach Policy Response Nodes, converting the wheel into an operational roadmap.
action.focus: Concrete countermeasures, civil defense, adaptive strategies, and mitigation design.
action.tool: Executive strategy workshops, defensive scenario planning, and war-gaming exercises.
action.output: Finland MoD cyber-defense plan linking response protocols to Baltic port and supply-chain attack scenarios.

wheelWorkshop.title: Build Your Futures Wheel
wheelWorkshop.sub: Enter the central issue and consequences for each order — the wheel draws automatically.
wheelWorkshop.cascadeHint: Click any 1st-order consequence to see details in the sidebar.
wheelWorkshop.sidebarSub: Select a 1st-order consequence to highlight its 2nd and 3rd order chain.
wheelWorkshop.errBranch: Add at least one 1st-order consequence with a title.

history.founder: John B. Robinson & Thomas L. Dreborg
history.title: Origins of Energy Backcasting
history.origin: Thomas Dreborg applied backcasting at Sweden's Energy Commission in the 1970s for alternative energy policy. John B. Robinson formalized the method in Energy Backcasting: A Proposed Method of Policy Analysis (Energy Policy, 1982), reframing energy planning at UBC from trend prediction to designing desirable futures.
history.problem: Forecasting can extend past trends but cannot design desirable alternative energy futures.
history.applications: Sweden's 1970s energy planning, net-zero transition (2045), Vancouver's Livable Region Plan, and EU long-term energy scenarios.

backcastPage.heroSub: Start from a normative future and work backward — mapping policies, barriers, and practical steps needed from today.
backcastPage.methodologyLead: Backcasting runs opposite to forecasting: first you name the desired future, then you walk backward and ask what path must have been taken.
methodologyNarrative: Robinson formalised the method in 1982, but the idea came from 1970s Sweden: when you want net-zero or a liveable city, extending past trends is not enough. The analyst assumes the goal is achieved — e.g. “net-zero by 2045” — and steps backward.

Keep four moves in mind: envision the desired future, map the gap from today, design transition policies, and build near- and long-term roadmaps. Forecasting asks “what happens if trends continue?” Backcasting asks “how do we reach the future we want?”
backcastPage.methodologyP1: Backcasting begins with a desired future goal, not today's trends. Analysts assume that goal is achieved, then ask what had to change to get there.
backcastPage.methodologyP2: Four core steps: (1) envision the desired future, (2) gap analysis against the present, (3) design policies and regulations for transition, (4) build an adaptive roadmap of near-, mid-, and long-term actions. Human agency replaces statistical determinism.
backcastPage.comparisonSub: Trend-driven planning versus choice-driven planning in large-scale strategy.
backcastPage.comparisonIntroP1: Robinson introduced backcasting in the late 1970s for alternative energy policy. He argued forecasting — tied to past data — cannot design structural change or normatively desirable futures.
backcastPage.comparisonIntroP2: Forecasting asks: "What happens if trends continue?" Backcasting asks: "How do we reach a desirable future, and what stands in the way?" It fits energy transition, environmental policy, and urban planning.

comparisonRows.forecast: Short-term linear trends with rich data
comparisonRows.backcast: Long-term structural crises and systemic gaps

backcastPage.swedenTitle: Case Study: Sweden's Path to Net Zero by 2045
backcastPage.swedenIntro: In 2016 Sweden used backcasting — not trend forecasting — to set "net zero by 2045 and fossil-free transport by 2030," then planned backward from that endpoint.

swedenSteps.title: Final reverse step (2040–2045)
swedenSteps.body: Ban sales of petrol and diesel cars, deploy heavy electric trucks, and replace coal coke with hydrogen in steelmaking.

swedenSteps.title: Mid-range step (2030–2040)
swedenSteps.body: Decarbonize steel (HYBRIT hydrogen project), electrify freight rail, and launch advanced biofuel certificate markets.

swedenSteps.title: Near-term step (2016–2030)
swedenSteps.body: High carbon tax, EV purchase incentives, and public–private investment in battery manufacturing (Northvolt).
